title: Business-area taxonomy is a committed, human-editable markdown file at context/org-structure.md
context: Story 022 adds a three-level cascading picker (Business Area -> Portfolio -> Squad) to the phase-4 profile review, replacing the free-prose 'Business area' section with structured data. The picker must be seeded with real org taxonomy and let the user add entries that persist. The PM left format and location to the architect with three hard constraints (022 AC1): the file lives in the repository, is readable by the Work HQ server at startup, is NOT inside docs/data/local/, and is NOT gitignored. The user's own framing was 'configurable via a .md file somewhere' — so the answer must be human-editable committed markdown, not a machine-only artefact. Two server functions read and write it: readTaxonomy (createServerFn GET, modelled on load-onboarding.ts) returns { businessAreas: Array<{ name; portfolios: Array<{ name; squads: string[] }> }> }; writeTaxonomy (createServerFn POST, modelled on save-onboarding.ts) accepts the same structure and persists user additions. Every existing server function (load-onboarding, save-onboarding, scaffold-files, read-bootstrap-prompt) resolves paths as resolve(process.cwd(), '..') + a repo-relative path, so the chosen path must sit under the repo root. The chosen triple must also flow into the profile-corrections handoff (file-contracts §4.3) as a single fact. The question: what file location + exact markdown shape best satisfies human-editability, committed-shared durability, server read/write, and consistency with the three-tier file layout (architecture §4.3)?

options.label: A
options.summary: Committed markdown at context/org-structure.md with `## <Business Area>` headings, `- <Portfolio>` bullets, and `  - <Squad>` nested bullets; readTaxonomy parses it, writeTaxonomy re-serialises it in the same canonical shape
pros: Human-editable committed markdown — exactly the '.md file somewhere' the user asked for; a non-technical user edits their org structure by hand in a plain nested list

Correct durability class and neighbourhood: it is org taxonomy, so it belongs beside context/org.md (also committed/durable-shared per architecture §4.1). A reader looking for 'my org structure' finds it next to org.md

Committed by default with NO gitignore change: .gitignore ignores only context/profile.md, context/me.md, context/active.md, example-profile.md — it does NOT ignore context/*.md broadly, so context/org-structure.md is committed automatically and satisfies 022 AC1 with zero new rules

Server read/write is trivial and consistent: resolve(process.cwd(), '../context/org-structure.md'), the identical idiom scaffold-files uses for context/*.md; readTaxonomy mirrors load-onboarding's read-and-parse, writeTaxonomy mirrors save-onboarding's mkdirSync+writeFileSync
Carries the standard §4.2 staleness header (# Org Structure · Updated: YYYY-MM-DD), so it is a first-class durable file like every other context file, not an untyped blob
cons: A markdown nested list needs a small purpose-built parser and serialiser (no JSON.parse for free) — mitigated: the grammar is 3 line-shapes and the writer emits one canonical form, so a round-trip of the seeded file is byte-idempotent

writeTaxonomy re-serialises the whole file rather than surgically appending, so non-canonical hand-formatting (tabs, extra blank lines) is normalised on the next write — acceptable and consistent with how scaffold-files always writes canonical full-file content

options.label: B
options.summary: JSON at docs/data/org-taxonomy.json — machine-clean structured data the server reads with JSON.parse and writes with JSON.stringify
pros: Zero parser/serialiser to write — the read/write is identical to load-onboarding/save-onboarding with no bespoke markdown grammar
The in-memory shape and on-disk shape are the same object, so writeTaxonomy is a one-line stringify
cons: Directly contradicts the user's explicit ask for a '.md file' — a non-technical user editing nested JSON (matching brackets, quoting, commas) is exactly the friction the Personal OS is built to avoid (architecture G2: non-technical maintainability)

docs/data/ currently holds only machine artefacts (adrs, features, stories, local runtime state) — org taxonomy is user-facing org context, so it is a poor neighbourhood; a user would not look in docs/data/ for 'my business areas'
Loses the §4.2 staleness-header convention and the diffable-prose reviewability that every other durable context file has

options.label: C
options.summary: A new `## Business areas` section appended inside context/org.md — no new file at all
pros: No new file; org.md is already committed (not gitignored), so 022 AC1's committed constraint is met
Keeps all org context in one place a reader already knows
cons: Write collision: context/org.md is authored by scaffold-files (manual path) and distilled/overwritten by the user's AI assistant from profile.md (bootstrap path, §1.2 / ADR-P6-007). A writeTaxonomy that edits org.md would fight the assistant's whole-file rewrites and the scaffold overwrite-protection heuristic — two writers, one file, guaranteed clobbering

Mixes two concerns with different lifecycles: org.md is sensitivity-gated free-text stakeholder/glossary content reviewed monthly; the taxonomy is a stable structured pick-list the picker mutates on every user addition. Parsing a strict nested list out of a free-text file the assistant reformats at will is brittle
Forces the taxonomy parser to tolerate arbitrary surrounding org.md prose, rather than owning a file whose entire grammar it controls

decision:
Adopt Option A. The business-area taxonomy is a committed, human-editable markdown file at context/org-structure.md.

PATH (exact): context/org-structure.md — resolved server-side as resolve(process.cwd(), '../context/org-structure.md'), the same idiom scaffold-files uses for context/*.md. It is committed by default: .gitignore ignores only context/profile.md, context/me.md, context/active.md, and example-profile.md — NOT context/*.md broadly — so NO gitignore change is made and the file is shared with the repo like context/org.md. It is not under docs/data/local/. This satisfies 022 AC1 verbatim.

EXACT MARKDOWN STRUCTURE (three line-shapes only):
  line 1  : `# Org Structure · Updated: YYYY-MM-DD` (real date, §4.2 staleness header)
  business area : a `## <Business Area>` heading
  portfolio     : a top-level `- <Portfolio>` bullet (zero leading whitespace)
  squad         : a nested `  - <Squad>` bullet (exactly two leading spaces)
Any line that is not the title, a `## ` heading, a `- ` portfolio bullet, or a `  - ` squad bullet (blank lines, an optional HTML guidance comment) is ignored by the parser.

FULL SEEDED CONTENT (the implementing developer commits this verbatim, refreshing the date to the ship date):
```
# Org Structure · Updated: 2026-07-19

<!-- Personal OS business-area taxonomy · file-contracts §1.4 / ADR-P6-010.
     Edit by hand to match your organisation. Shape:
       ## <Business Area>
       - <Portfolio>
         - <Squad>
     The onboarding review picker reads this file and writes your additions
     back to it in this same shape. -->

## Residential
- Portfolio 1
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
- Portfolio 2
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3

## Commercial
- Portfolio 1
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
- Portfolio 2
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3

## New Homes and Media
- Portfolio 1
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
- Portfolio 2
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3

## Experience and Platforms
- Portfolio 1
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
- Portfolio 2
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3

## Financial Services
- Portfolio 1
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
- Portfolio 2
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3

## Product Operations
- Portfolio 1
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
- Portfolio 2
  - Squad name 1
  - Squad name 2
  - Squad name 3
```

PARSE RULES (readTaxonomy, createServerFn GET, strict:false, modelled on load-onboarding.ts): read the file; if absent, return { businessAreas: [] }. Split into lines. Ignore the title line and any non-matching line. On a `## ` line, open a new business area { name: <text after '## '>, portfolios: [] }. On a top-level `- ` line (no leading space), open a new portfolio { name, squads: [] } under the current business area. On a `  - ` line (leading whitespace), push the squad text onto the current portfolio's squads. Trim trailing whitespace on every captured value. The parser is deliberately LENIENT on input whitespace (a tab or 2+ spaces both count as a nested squad) so hand-edits survive.

WRITE-BACK RULES (writeTaxonomy, createServerFn POST, modelled on save-onboarding.ts): the client sends the FULL { businessAreas } structure (not a delta — matching how save-onboarding sends the whole state). The handler validates with zod, then re-serialises the whole file in the ONE canonical shape above: refresh the `# Org Structure · Updated: <today>` header, preserve the guidance comment, then emit each business area as `## <name>`, each portfolio as `- <name>`, each squad as `  - <name>`, with one blank line between business areas. mkdirSync(dirname)+writeFileSync, exactly like save-onboarding. This is full-file canonical write, NOT surgical text-patching: surgical append was rejected because (a) AC2 passes the full structure, not a diff, and (b) no server function in this codebase does in-place text surgery — scaffold-files/save-onboarding all write canonical whole files. 'Preserving user formatting' is therefore bounded and honest: CONTENT and ORDER (all areas/portfolios/squads, in list order, plus any user-added entry appended to its parent) are preserved exactly; only non-canonical whitespace (tabs, stray blank lines) is normalised. A round-trip of the seeded file is byte-idempotent because the seed is already canonical.

SELECTION REPRESENTATION (ratifies the PM convention in 022 AC5, unchanged): the chosen triple persists in onboarding-state.json answers.editedSections['Business area'] as ONE Fact { id: 'business-area-selection', decision: 'edited', text: '', editedText: '<BusinessArea> > <Portfolio> > <Squad>' }. Partial selections drop trailing levels: BA+Portfolio only -> '<BusinessArea> > <Portfolio>'; BA only -> '<BusinessArea>'. Through the standard fact-level handoff path (§4.3) this emits exactly one bullet under the `**Business area**` section heading: `- <BusinessArea> > <Portfolio> > <Squad>`. The ` > ` separator (space-gt-space) is ratified as the pinned join token — it matches nothing else in the fact grammar so it round-trips unambiguously, and buildProfileCorrectionsMarkdown needs no special-casing (it is an ordinary decision:'edited' fact).

SEEDING OWNERSHIP: the implementing developer for story 022 creates and commits context/org-structure.md with the seeded content above as part of the story. It is a committed starter every clone inherits.

CONCURRENCY: single-user local app, one workspace, one wizard tab. Last-write-wins is acceptable and explicitly chosen — there is no multi-writer scenario for this file (unlike org.md, which the assistant also writes; that collision is precisely why the taxonomy gets its own file). No locking, no merge.
consequences: New committed file context/org-structure.md (seeded by story 022's developer). New server functions work-hq/src/server/read-taxonomy.ts (readTaxonomy) and work-hq/src/server/write-taxonomy.ts (writeTaxonomy), each carrying a small pure parse/serialise helper for the 3-shape grammar. file-contracts.md gains a new §1.4 documenting this file (path, structure, parse/write rules, seeding owner, gitignore=committed) at v1.3, and §4.3 gains the business-area single-bullet note. No new gitignore rule. No change to scaffold-files, load-onboarding, or save-onboarding. Forecloses nothing: if the taxonomy ever needs attributes per node (owner, status) the file could migrate to frontmatter or JSON via a superseding ADR, but the flat nested list is sufficient for the pick-list use. The parser owns a file whose entire grammar it controls, so it never has to tolerate foreign prose (the reason Option C was rejected).
costCapacity: No new recurring cost. readTaxonomy is one local file read on the phase-4 route load; writeTaxonomy is one local file write on user confirmation of a new entry. No metered operations, no MCP, no network. Consistent with AD-1 (file-based persistence) and AD-4 (no load-bearing MCP).

drift.designed: context/org-structure.md committed markdown (## area / - portfolio / 2-space nested - squad); readTaxonomy GET + writeTaxonomy POST full-file canonical re-serialise; selection persisted as a single business-area-selection fact joined with ' > '.
